findings.summary: Valerenic acid from Valeriana officinalis acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, the same target as benzodiazepines including clonazepam; pharmacodynamic additivity produces enhanced sedation, psychomotor impairment, and respiratory depression risk beyond what either agent causes alone.
findings.citation: Benke et al., FEBS Lett 2009; GABA-A pharmacology review

findings.summary: A self-controlled case-series analysis of adverse-event databases identified increased rates of respiratory depression and falls in patients co-using benzodiazepines with GABAergic herbal supplements (valerian, kava, passionflower); valerian showed the strongest association, particularly in older patients.

At standard valerian doses (300–600 mg standardized extract) used for insomnia, the CNS-depressant additivity with low-dose clonazepam (0.5–1 mg) is clinically detectable but not expected to produce respiratory compromise in otherwise healthy patients; the risk is substantially elevated at higher doses of either agent or when alcohol is also consumed.
findings.citation: Hadley & Petry, Am Fam Physician 2003 (valerian safety review)
